Originally Answered: What kind of alien technology do we think advanced aliens have?

Hmm. Attempting some Physics based speculation, I think they would have to have advanced technology to survive past the age of their planet's habitability. They would be so advanced their technology would have technology, i.e. self-improving, self-replicating machines & automated everything. They would be beyond biology in that sense, but to get to that point they would have to completely master genetic engineering as well.
So basically they could create any possible form of life including immortal life out of raw materials, and their machines could take any possible form and any possible s

An advanced alien civilization’s technologies depend on its stage (energy use, materials, computation) and environment (stellar type, planet, vacuum habitats). Below is a structured taxonomy of plausible technologies, ordered roughly from near-future extrapolations to extreme, speculative capabilities tied to physical limits.

Energy capture, storage, and manipulation
Stellar-scale harnessing: Dyson swarms/shells, orbital rings, stellar engines (Shkadov thrusters) to move stars.
Fusion and matter–antimatter power: dense power sources for habitats, propulsion, and computation.
Zero-point and vac

Technology is a reflection of the societies that it comes from. It is a reflection of their wants and needs. We can state every piece of technology that is considered “Sci Fi” and so forth, but we would not be able to give an actual answer unless we know about them as they are.

Their intelligence could work on a totally different pattern, they may have different living needs, different thought processes. All of these would change the technology that they could have. Unless we knew about them, how would we know about their technology.

Let’s be real — the usual advice about skipping lattes or canceling Netflix is a joke. The cost of living has gone feral, and a $6 coffee isn’t the reason most of us are broke.

If you actually want to survive right now, you need tricks that feel a little off the rails — like slashing bills you didn’t even know were ripping you off, or getting paid for stuff you already waste time on anyway. There’s really no real way to even guess at that without knowing more about the specific aliens that developed the technology because technology is intrinsic to the culture that creates it.

Some of the basics might be the same as a tech that we humans develop for doing the same thing but what it looks like and how it works would likely be different.

So you might be able to make broad categorical guesses but specifics would be impossible to know with any certainty…

For instance if they’ve made the trip to our planet they would either have some form of FTL star drive, Suspended animation, or really

That depends on how far advanced they are. If like 6 months ahead of us they may have season 4 of STRANGER THINGS.

But seriously, assuming they are like us ~ a big assumption ~ they would have to have survived war, climate change, their own brand of racism (the purple zops may hate the pink zeems) to get really advanced. At least we’d better hope that they did before they mastered interstellar travel; ask African blacks, Aztecs, Native Americans, or indigenous Australians what it’s like to meet advanced civilizations. Or, they may be as uninterested in conquering us as we would be be of taking

content:
2 Quantitative classification: Kardashev’s scale
3 Qualitative classification of ETCs
4 Implications for our notion of ETCs
5 Predictions and implications for the SETI strategies
6 Summary and conclusions
A qualitative classification of extraterrestrial civilizations
Valentin D. Ivanov1,Juan Carlos Beamín2,Claudio Cáceres3,4andDante Minniti3,5
1European Southern Observatory, 
Karl-Schwarzschild-Str. 2, 
85748 
Garching bei München, 
Germanye-mail:This email address is being protected from spambots. You need JavaScript enabled to view it.2Núcleo de Astroquímica y Astrofísica, Instituto de Ciencias Químicas Aplicadas, Facultad de Ingeniería, Universidad Autónoma de Chile, 
Av. Pedro de Valdívia 425, 
Santiago, 
Chile3Departamento de Ciencias Físicas, Facultad de Ciencias Exactas, Universidad Andrés Bello, 
Av. Fernandez Concha 700, 
Las Condes, 
Santiago, 
Chile4Núcleo Milenio Formación Planetaria – NPF, Universidad de Valparaíso, 
Av. Gran Bretaña 1111, Playa Ancha, 
Casilla 
5030, 
Valparaíso, Chile5Vatican Observatory, 
V00120 
Vatican City State, Italy
Received:28 
January 
2020Accepted:26 
May 
2020
Context.Interest in searches for extraterrestrial civilizations (ETCs) has been boosted in recent decades by the discovery of thousands of exoplanets.
Aims.We turn to the classification of ETCs for new considerations that may help to design better strategies for searching for ETCs.
Methods.This study is based on analogies with our own biological, historical, technological, and scientific development. We took a basic taxonomic approach to ETCs and investigated the implications of the new classification on ETC evolution and observational patterns. Finally, we used the quantitative scheme of Kardashev and considered its implications on the searches for ETCs as a counter example to our qualitative classification.
Results.We propose a classification based on the abilities of ETCs to modify and integrate with their environments: Class 0 uses the environment as it is, Class 1 modifies the environment to fit its needs, Class 2 modifies itself to fit the environment, and a Class 3 ETC is fully integrated with the environment. Combined with the classical Kardashev scale, our scheme forms a two-dimensional method for interpreting ETC properties.
Conclusions.The new framework makes it obvious that the available energy is not a unique measure of ETC progress: it may not even correlate with how well that energy is used. The possibility for progress without increased energy consumption implies a lower detectability, so in principle the existence of a Kardashev Type III ETC in the Milky Way cannot be ruled out. This reasoning weakens the Fermi paradox, allowing for the existence of advanced, yet not energy hungry, low-detectability ETCs. The integration of ETCs with the environment will make it impossible to tell technosignatures and natural phenomena apart. Therefore, the most likely opportunity for SETI searches to find advanced ETCs is to look for beacons, specifically set up by them for young civilizations like ours (if they would want to do that remains a matter of speculation). The other SETI window of opportunity is to search for ETCs at technological level similar to ours. To rephrase the famous saying of Arthur Clarke, sufficiently advanced civilizations are indistinguishable from nature.
Key words:extraterrestrial intelligence / astrobiology / history and philosophy of astronomy
On a fundamental level, the search for extraterrestrial civilizations (ETCs) is motivated by scientific curiosity. We want to understand how intelligent life and intelligence arise and evolve, to compare biologies, histories, and social structures that have developed completely independently from each other. Undoubtedly, contact with an ETC carries potential risks (Neal 2014), but there are also indications that it may have a stimulating effect on humanity (Kwon & Bercovici 2017). In the long run, the transfer of new knowledge from fundamental sciences to industry is likely to induce fast economic growth, and on a purely psychological level we will have for the first time a truly external – with respect to the human kind – scale to measure up ourselves and our achievements.
However, all these promising prospects ultimately require a successful search for extraterrestrial intelligence (SETI). The SETI programs in the last half century have been fruitless. One possibility is that the Universe is empty, but the commonly found ingredients of life (at least as we know it) cast some doubts on this hypothesis. Another option is that the ETCs are extremely rare. Therefore, success is just a matter of time and increased sensitivity of the techniques we are already applying. Without listing all the possibilities for the silentium universi, let us consider the possibility that our search strategies may be wrong. Indeed, some time agoBradbury et al. (2011)pointed to certain caveats in our strategies: SETI mostly remains an effort isolated from the wider astronomical and astrobiological studies, and the SETI proponents refuse to adopt a broader multidisciplinary approach and to consider important criticism.
So far, the dominant SETI approach, going back toCocconi & Morrison (1959), relies on (i) the willingness of ETCs to be detected or (ii) their unwillingness or failure to control their own energy waste (that we could detect). In the former case we are searching for radio beacons set up with the purpose of being visible to other ETCs, and in the latter for the emission that would leak into space in the course of regular radio communications. The transfer of the searches to other ranges of the electromagnetic spectrum does not make a fundamental difference (Fabian 1977;Wright et al. 2014a).
Kardashev (1964)evaluated the feasibility of these approaches in radio and as a by-product developed a quantitative scheme to measure the stage of an ETC’s advancement via the total amount of energy they have at their disposal. Undoubtedly, Kardashev’s classification is still useful for SETI because it helps to define benchmark energy capabilities of the ETCs and thus to set up sensitivity requirements for the SETI equipment. However, some new considerations have arisen in recent years. For example,Benford et al. (2010a,b) argued about cost-optimized means of interstellar communication, and proposed some strategies to that effect (see more arguments for cost-saving inDavies & Wagner 2013).Kardashev (1964)had not taken these into account. He implicitly makes the assumption that unlimited resources are available to the ETCs, allowing unconstrained growth of the energy production and consumption, at least up to a galactic scale. Furthermore, the estimates of Kardashev are upper limits that give the maximum energy available for interstellar communication for the given level of ETC development.
Our historic and modern experience can hardly support unconstrained growth. Therefore, it is compelling to re-examine the ETC classification as a guiding tool for SETI strategies, aiming to optimize them and to reach a new priority scale for the different search methods. The next section reviews Kardashev’s classification and its implications.Sections 3,4, and5describe a new quantitative scale proposed here and its implications.Section 6summarizes this work.
2 Quantitative classification: Kardashev’s scale
Kardashev (1964)introduced a classification scheme for ETCs based on the energy available to them. This is a quantitative approach, well justified in the context of that study, aimed to determine the technical feasibility of the communication between civilizations. He considered an isotropic radio emission and estimated that sending information with a data rate of 3 × 105bits s−1at distance of ~3 Mpc required transmitting power of 4 × 1033erg s−1, comparable to the total solar luminosity. The adopted limit of ~3 Mpc is just below the distances to the M81 and Cen A groups of galaxies and encompasses a l

However, all these promising prospects ultimately require a successful search for extraterrestrial intelligence (SETI). Therefore, success is just a matter of time and increased sensitivity of the techniques we are already applying. Dr. Elizabeth Fernandez is the host of SparkDialog Podcasts (sparkdialog.com), which covers the intersection of science and society. She has a PhD in astrophysics from the University of Texas at Austin and has worked around the world in both astronomy and in science and society. She currently specializes in how science and technology are impacting our lives.
There are specific marks on a planet that intelligent life may leave. To find technological civilizations among the stars, we just need to know what to look for.

Are we alone in the Universe? Are there other technological civilizations out there? Many astronomers are looking for biosignatures, or certain observational signs that life may exist on a planet. Others try to compute how many intelligent civilizations are out there using assumptions about what we know about Earth-like planets and life itself. A new grant, however, aims to answer this question directly - to actually look for signs of a technologically advanced society through “technosignatures”. they had calculated how many intelligent civilizations may exist in our Galaxy. They came up with a rather exact number - InternalLink:https://www.forbes.com/sites/jamiecartereurope/2020/06/15/there-are-36-intelligent-alien-civilizations-in-our-galaxy-say-scientists
InternalLink:https://www.forbes.com/sites/startswithabang/2020/06/16/36-alien-civilizations-in-the-milky-way-the-science-behind-a-ridiculous-headline
. The Drake Equation is a way to calculate how many civilizations there are in the Galaxy which we may be able to communicate with. It depends on the rate of star formation, the fraction of those stars with planets, the number of those that can develop an ecosystem, the fraction of these where life actually appears, the fraction that develops intelligent life, and the fraction of those that develop the ability to communicate into space. All of this is multiplied by the lifetime of the civilization itself. We have a pretty good idea about the first few terms of the Drake Equation. We know the star formation rate, and we’re even beginning to understand how many planets there are that have liquid water on them - and are potentially habitable. we still have no idea how often life arises, how often it evolves into intelligent life, or even how long this civilization lasts before blowing itself to smithereens or killing itself off due to climate change. The Drake Equation is useful to understand what conditions are needed for an intelligent civilization, but it may be premature to use it to get anything more concrete.
. These are signs that a planet hosts life - microbial to intelligent. The concept is that the presence of life changes a planet - and these changes may be observable to our telescopes. This could be a (due to metabolism of life). It could also be the way light reflects off the planet, indicating the presence of vegetation or something like microbial mats. It could also be time-dependent changes to the planet. Finding life - any type of life - would be game-changing. Philosophically, it will indicate to us that we are indeed not alone in the Universe. And specifically, it would narrow down one of those numbers in the Drake equation - the number of planets that actually form life.
A new grant, recently funded by NASA, attempts to answer the question - what would a technological civilization look like to us, across the Galaxy? The of the University of Rochester, along with Jacob-Haqq Misra of Blue Marble Space, Manasvi Lingam from the Florida Institute of Technology, Avi Loeb of Harvard, and Jason Eright from Pennsylvania State University. This grant stands out because it is the first time . It is also the first NASA non-radio technosignature awarded - which means that the scientists are not just looking at the radio band, as many SETI searches so far. To figure this out, we must make an important realization - alien life will use the same types of energy we use. They are confined to the same laws of chemistry and physics we are. “There are only so many forms of energy in the universe. Aliens are not magic,” . “A young civilization... is only going to have so many kinds of energy modes available to it.” Aliens would have to get their energy the same places we would - burning things, the movement of their atmosphere or oceans, the light of their star. could be a product of industry. Chemicals like this would leave a spectral signature in the atmosphere of the planet, which we may be able

Elizabeth Fernandez is the host of SparkDialog Podcasts (sparkdialog.com), which covers the intersection of science and society.
causal_sentences: It depends on the rate of star formation, the fraction of those stars with planets, the number of those that can develop an ecosystem, the fraction of these where life actually appears, the fraction that develops intelligent life, and the fraction of those that develop the ability to communicate into space.

We know the star formation rate, and we’re even beginning to understand how many planets there are that have liquid water on them - and are potentially habitable. we still have no idea how often life arises, how often it evolves into intelligent life, or even how long this civilization lasts before blowing itself to smithereens or killing itself off due to climate change.
This could be a (due to metabolism of life).
This grant stands out because it is the first time .

in: Powers, Technology-based Powers, Alien Powers
Alien Technology

Alien Technology
The Yautja/Predators (Predator/Aliens vs. Predator) are a highly advanced technological species, possessing various devices and weaponry geared towards hunting the worst creatures the universe has to offer.
Power/Ability to:
Create and/or possess alien technology.

The power to create and/or use technology of alien origin. Variation of Extraterrestrial Object and Exotic Technology. Combination of Extraterrestrial Force Manipulation and Technological Constructs.

Contents
1 Also Called
2 Capabilities
3 Applications
4 Associations
5 Limitations
6 Known Users
7 Known Items
8 Gallery
8.1 Spacecrafts, Structures, Items
8.2 Movies/Films
8.3 Anime/Manga
8.4 Live Television
8.5 Video Games
8.6 Web Animation/Comics/Series/Original
9 Videos
9.1 Fights & Feats
9.2 Analysis & Discussions
9.3 Music
Also Called
Extraterrestrial/Xeno Technology
Capabilities

The user can create and/or use technology of extraterrestrial nature, containing powers and abilities of alien origins that can make them more advanced and more powerful than earthly/mundane technology.

Applications
Exotic Technology Manipulation
Exotic Data Manipulation
Supernatural Properties Manipulation
Supernatural Science/Meta Science
Technological Psionics
Technology Manipulation
Advanced Technology/Ultimate Technology (Varies)
Extraterrestrial Force Manipulation
Alien Weaponry
Foreign Energy/Cosmic Energy Manipulation
High-Tech Alien Exoskeleton
Xeno Proficiency
Associations
Exotic Technology
Extraterrestrial Object
Extraterrestrial Robot
Limitations
Technology Immunity
Alien Technology is likely going to require a lot of power, like most technology does.
Technology Manipulation may be able to stop the technology, but due to the complexity of the machine, this may be difficult.
It may require at least Enhanced Intelligence to understand the technology.

Gallery
The Biotech nodes called Guyver Units (Bioboosted Armor Guyver) are purely genetic-based enhancers that give the user augmented physical characteristics based on the potential of its host.
Despite their martial ways and brutal lifestyles, Saiyans (Dragon Ball) were advanced in their technology, though part of it was stolen from other races.
Despite their simple lifestyles, Namekians (Dragon Ball) are far more advanced than they seem, having built spaceships of their own and travelled from the demon realm into space.
Frieza's race (Dragon Ball)
The Time Lords (Doctor Who)
Daleks (Doctor Who)
Zygons (Doctor Who) use bio-organic technology.
Axos (Doctor Who) is a living, bio-organic ship.
Forerunners (Halo)
Galactus (Marvel Comics)
Skrulls (Marvel Comics)
The Kree (Marvel Comics)
Shi'ar (Marvel Comics)
The Mon Calamari (Star Wars) are some of the best starship designers and builders in the galaxy, with even modified luxury vessels of theirs being comparable to some of the Empire's finest warships.
The Chiss (Star Wars), having developed in the isolated Unknown Regions far away from most civilizations, utilize different technologies than the rest of galactic society.
The extragalactic Yuuzhan Vong (Star Wars Legends) grow all of their technology organically and view conventional mechanical technology as "blasphemous."
The Silentium (Star Wars Legends) are a highly advanced civilization of droids from another galaxy.
The Gems of the Gem Homeworld (Steven Universe) possess technology so advanced they are capable of creating other gems with the resources available, preserving and maintaining human life, teleporting warp pads, and even space travel that can transcend normal laws of physics by bending reality.
Dr. Eggman (Sonic the Hedgehog) piloting the Nega-Wisp Armor, a potent machine powered by the combined Hyper-go-on of the Wisps.
Created by the extraterrestrial Ancients, the Titans (Sonic Frontiers) are supremely powerful robots, capable of easily matching the might of Super Sonic.
The Alterans/Ancients/Lanteans (Stargate series) were one of the earliest evolved races in the known universe and also one of the most advanced...
...their greatest achievement, recognized by everyone, being the creation of the entire Stargate Network.
The Asgard (Stargate series) were one of the most advanced races in the known universe, rivaling (and in some areas maybe even surpassing) even the Ancients.
The Wraith (Stargate) are an extragalactic species noted for using very "alien" organic technology.
The Progenitors (Star Trek) were the first and most advanced intelligent humanoid life to have evolved in the Milky Way galaxy and were responsible for seeding countless planets with primitive life.
Species 8472 (Star Trek: Voyager) is an extradimensional race that uses organic technology so alien the Borg cannot assimilate it.
Almeracians (DC Comics)
Brainiac (DC Comics) possesses technology so advanced that it can tap into and replicate the effects of the magical realms or even steal it for himself.
Kryptonians (DC Comics)
New Gods (DC Comics)
The Lantern Corps (DC Comics) have the greatest technology in the universe.
Engineers (Alien franchise) created most life in the universe and appear to have created the Xenomorphs as well.
The Foot Mechs (Teenage Mutant Ninja Turtles 2003 TV series) were based on the Utrom exosuits and had advanced weaponry, but because they were formed around an Utrom biochip, recovered after the destruction of the TCRI building, and were too complicated to replicate with earthly technology, only nine Foot Mech prototypes could be constructed.
Cybertronians (Transformers)
Kryptonians (My Adventures w

key_facts: Technology Manipulation may be able to stop the technology, but due to the complexity of the machine, this may be difficult.

The Foot Mechs (Teenage Mutant Ninja Turtles 2003 TV series) were based on the Utrom exosuits and had advanced weaponry, but because they were formed around an Utrom biochip, recovered after the destruction of the TCRI building, and were too complicated to replicate with earthly technology, only nine Foot Mech prototypes could be constructed.

Technology Manipulation may be able to stop the technology, but due to the complexity of the machine, this may be difficult.
It may require at least Enhanced Intelligence to understand the technology.

The Foot Mechs (Teenage Mutant Ninja Turtles 2003 TV series) were based on the Utrom exosuits and had advanced weaponry, but because they were formed around an Utrom biochip, recovered after the destruction of the TCRI building, and were too complicated to replicate with earthly technology, only nine Foot Mech prototypes could be constructed.
